sentences.text: Good afternoon, Stella.
My name was [?
Labol ?] Leyson.
Labol.
My Hebrew was [?
Arya. ?]
I was born in 1929.
September 15.
I was born in a small town in eastern Poland called Narewka.
His name was Moshe.
And my mother's name was Anna.
He was a machinist.
He was tool and dye maker.
He started in Narewka, and then he eventually transferred with a company to a place called Krakow, in Poland.
Oh, from Narewka, Krakow was approximately 350 kilometers.
Well, not very often.
He-- he actually as a young man left Narewka to seek his fortune in Krakow, because in a small town like Narewka, there was not too many opportunities for a young man at his age.
He was in his 30s, and so when the company moved, he was asked to relocate.
And so he left Narewka and his family and went to establish himself in the big city.
Well, it was-- would have been very difficult to-- for a young man in his 30s just getting started to take his family to a big city.

It was-- besides, in those days, people didn't move very much, and my mother thought that it would be easier for her with five children to stay in Narewka, where she had her-- her parents, my grandparents, and my father's parents.
So we children were looked after by both grandparents, and so it was much easier that way.
It was--
It was thought that it was-- wasn't going to be very long before he could bring his family.
Well, he was a-- an easygoing kind of father.
He-- he encouraged us, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was not of an aggressive kind of person.
Patient.
Yes, he did.

Yes, he was very proud of that, because the family originally was-- his own family was not a rich family, and each-- each member of the family had to work hard to-- to provide for themselves and for their own families.
So he was a proud provider, and he provided very well.
Yes, he did.
You know, in Poland in the '30s, there weren't many denominations of-- of the Jewish religion.
There weren't-- there was Orthodox, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was not exactly Orthodox.
But his family was.
So we used to go to a temple in-- in Krakow once we moved to Krakow, and he used to take us to--
In Narewka there was one synagogue, and everyone was Jewish.
There was no such thing as something else.
My mother's name was Anna.
Her maiden name is Golner.
She was from not Narewka.

Her family-- not everyone in her family was from Narewka, but it was-- people didn't travel very far, so you were either from Narewka or the surrounding area, about a circle of maybe 40, 50 kilometers.
There were five children in our family.
Yes, she was.
Yeah, that's right.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, well, I think she did.
We-- we turned out OK.
Yes, she was strict, but she was a very kind woman.
But she had four boys and-- and a girl, and some of the boys needed some discipline.
So that's what she did.
But we were looked after by the grandparents as well, so it was really an extended family and warm.
And either my father's side they sort of lived on either side of the street from us.
And so when we moved-- when we went to my father's parents, it was a warm place to be.
And in my recollection, when I was young, living in Narewka was quite comfortable.
Narewka was the total population was approximately 2,000.
About half.
About half the population in Narewka was Jewish.
A town.
Well, it's-- it's a little difficult.
All the houses, of course, were wooden, and they all had gardens in the front and in the back.
We grew vegetables.
For example, in our home, we grew vegetables in the back of the house and flowers in the front.
And it was quite pretty, actually.
It was very quiet.
Simple, very simple town.
Water was drawn from a well.
No inside plumbing.
And transportation was either walking or by horse and wagon.
No, I didn't see a big city until our family finally joined my father in Krakow.
That was in 1938.
I was just going to turn eight-- nine years old.
Well, I was probably about two years old, because he moved when he was a young man, in approximately 1932 or something like that.
In 1932, he was 34 years old.
Well, he-- he used to come when he had his vacations.
During vacation time, he would come in and used to be about every six months or so.
A couple of weeks.
It was a big occasion when my father came.
Absolutely, and not only that, but it was sort of a big occasion for the whole town.
Well, a-- their own native son from a small town comes to visit the little town, people who don't travel very much.
It was an occasion.
Mhm.
He did.
He used to bring gifts, yeah.
My-- well, practical things.
Well, he would send us shoes and school bags.
They were more like briefcases in those days.
And I recall when he brought us these briefcases, and they were various sizes.
And I only saw two of them.
And so I thought, well, the one was for my older brothers, and I wasn't going to get one.
But there were some inside.
I did.
Tell me before we continue.
What were the names of your brothers and sister?
My oldest brother was Hershel.
And then next to him was [?
Salig. ?] His name was [INAUDIBLE].
We called him [?
Salig ?] for short.
And then my sister, who's now known as Aviva.
What was she called as a child?
Pescha.
And then my brother David and me.
I was the youngest.
We all went to the same school.
There was only one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] public school, and it actually went to the sixth grade and then after that one had to travel to another-- a larger city like [INAUDIBLE] start to continue.
Well, I was not such a good student.
My-- my brothers and sisters were better.
But I was-- I had an advantage because whatever they learned I learned as well.
So when I went-- when it was my time to go to school, I had already learned some of those things.
So I had a little advantage that way.
But not because I was smarter.
I just-- I knew it.
Well, it was standard for a Jewish boy to go to public school in the morning and go to cheder in the afternoon.
And there was standard procedure.
Well, we-- we studied the [HEBREW].
Yeah, [?
Poluka, ?] sure.
I don't remember.
It's just known as [?
Poluka, ?] and everybody knew him as that-- by that name.
It was in-- at his home, at the-- at the teacher's home in his-- I recall, his front room was set up with tables and chairs and long benches.
And we sat there, and you probably see typical pictures of a cheder in-- of the-- of that period, and when I see them, I see myself.
Yes, but not every day.
I mean not throughout the day.
Only when I went cheder.
There were probably about 15 or 20 of us, various ages.
Yeah, I liked-- I like stories, so it was very interesting for me to learn [HEBREW].
Well, when I was bar mitzvah, I was in camp by that time.
So my bar mitzvah was just that.
My camp was my bar mitzvah.
No, but I'm-- I'm planning to have one.
Yeah.
I attended a bar mitzvah not too long ago, someone who was approximately my age, and he celebrated his 13-- his bar mitzvah exactly 55 years after his 13th birthday.
So I still have a chance.
Well, of course.
But I-- I act as though I had a bar mitzvah anyway.
Well, in that when I am called to the Torah, I, you know, I recite the prayers in the same way that anybody who had bar mitzvah.
Yes, I do.
[LAUGHS]
Well, I don't know about that.
Yes.
We-- we-- we had a-- for our city, it was kind of a progressive cheder.
We didn't just learn the Torah.
We learned arithmetic and other secular things.
So in that way, we were kind of advanced.
And we learned songs, folk songs that we used to sing.
It-- it was a good idea.
I guess he was a really good teacher, because he knew what children need and what motivates them, so.
Oh no.
It's so-- it's so traditional.
You know.
We learned [INAUDIBLE] on mandolin and [INAUDIBLE], which was for us a-- the place where we were, really.
You know, we were doing the same-- the things that we were singing about.
Yeah.
Well, in Narewka, along the-- Narewka-- the city of Narewka, there is a river.
And for us, this was a-- a place to be.
We-- in the summertime, we go-- we went swimming there.
In the wintertime, we skated on it.

And it was just-- there was a meadow between our-- the buildings or the homes and-- and the river, and as children, I recall as a very pleasant time to have-- to be able to just run out there and go swimming.
In the summertime, we mostly ran around barefoot, you know, really free souls.
And as I said earlier, we had our grandparents on either side of the street, and we had friends and uncles and aunts and-- who lived almost across the street from us.

Well, you know, the-- during my period of time, the-- my memory, the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews was very congenial in that there was sort of a symbiotic relationship in that there was an exchange in commerce and exchange in goods and production, for example.
We had neighbors who were non-Jewish neighbors and as children we played together.
Well, anti-Semitism in Poland, especially in eastern Poland, was a way of life.
It-- I-- it was so strong and it was so common that we just accepted it, and we-- we just worked around it.
Well, for example, we had this woman who lived almost across the street from us, and she used to chase around because she didn't want us to cross-- to walk in front of her house.
So when we would try to walk in front of her house, she would chase us away.
And so we had to go across the street to go past her house.
But that's OK.
We called her names for that, and that was good.
Well, we were Jewish kids.
She didn't want any Jews in front of her house.
Oh, sure.
Oh, no.
Oh no.
It was so-- such a common thing that if one had to-- wanted to do this with everyone who was anti-Semitic, you would be spending your life just speaking to people about it.
So.
Yeah, it was something that.
You know, during our period of time there weren't any atrocities committed and anti-Semitism wasn't carried very far, other than some little incidences like chasing us away from her house.
And she was just a nasty woman and that's all.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But before that, before my-- my time, during my parent's time as young people there were atrocities committed against Jews.
There were pogroms.
Yes, there were pogroms and there were rioting.
In Narewka, yes.
Absolutely.
They had to go hide and-- to escape being beaten up or worse.
No, I don't think so.
At least I don't recall that.
You know, we considered our place where we lived our home, and there was no-- not much thought of moving.
However, there were no opportunities for young people in a small town like that.
My father, you know, since he had the opportunity to move out of Narewka, and it was-- the young people wanted to move naturally.
They looked for-- tried to look for opportunities.
My father left about-- in '32 or '33?
About-- I was eight years old.
Oh, it was exciting.
I-- I-- I was sorry to leave my grandparents, but I was excited to come to Krakow and to live with-- where my father was.
Yes, as soon as we arrived in Krakow, we were sent to school and continued our education.
I don't recall the name of the school.
I should-- I should remember that.
It was a public school, and it was within walking distance from where we lived.
Oh yes, sure.
It was-- we lived on [INAUDIBLE], a street called [INAUDIBLE], which means industrial city-- industrial street.
Not a-- not a-- it was a good neighborhood, but it wasn't a high-class neighborhood.
It was really an industrial area where my father worked, near his-- the company where he worked.
And the housing was actually factory housing.
Not immediately in the-- in the neighborhood.
There was one at the end of the street, but not many-- not many Jewish neighbors in that area.
That was not a Jewish area.
We were-- were were across the river.
Krakow is a city that's divided by a river, and on one side of the-- of the river, it's actually called by a different name.
Podgorze.
And Krakow was on the other side.
And we lived in Podgorze, and the Jewish section-- the Jewish sector of Krakow was across the river in Krakow, in the other side-- on the other side.
Not really.
We were too busy trying to-- to acclimate ourselves to-- to the new environment.
And, you know, I was looking at the tall buildings, the first time I ever saw a tall building or a street car.
So there was no time for any of that.
Well, quite a bit in that my mother was isolated by then.
You know, she-- in Narewka, she was surrounded by her family and friends.
And when she moved to Krakow, she's just had to start all over again.
It's like practically moving to a new country.
Not-- not much.
She was-- she was a strong woman.
She-- she was efficient and hard-working, so that when she had-- when something had to be done, she just went to it and did it.
She may-- may have fallen apart later on, you know, but when it was-- the thing had to be done, she-- she just went ahead and did it.
Well, we continued until the war broke out.
It broke-- we-- we had only been there one year and the war broke out in 1939 and Germans came in.
When that started, of course, it was, as we know now, was the beginning of a very dark, dark period in-- in history of Europe and in-- in our personal history.

Well, as soon as the Germans came in, their campaign against Jews was immediately apparent, although in the beginning, it looked like just occasional drunken soldiers bored with being-- you know, just bored with army life and just having fun with Jews, beating them up or making them do things that are--
Oh yes, sure.
And dragging them down the street making and making them clean the streets or kneel or whatever just to humiliate them.
But as time went on, it was not long before we knew that there was a definite campaign that they're-- they were waging against the Jewish population.
Because the laws were being passed almost daily.
Well, immediately, as soon as the Germans came in, we were immediately thrown out of school.
Jewish children couldn't go to public school.
Well, you know the immediate feeling as a boy I recall I was, well, I don't have to go to school.
But then that only lasted for 10 seconds.
Because there was everything that was going to go on in school was passing me by.
Well, because my father's work and his life is a middle-class level of-- of income, as soon as the war broke out, his savings and his [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] security disappeared immediately.
So really as soon as-- as the war broke out we had to start looking around to see how we could help each other and help the family to survive.
That was not a possibility.
That option was not there.
When the Germans occupied Poland in 1939, the Russians occupied the eastern part of Poland.
When that happened, Narewka became under the control of the Soviets, so there was no way to-- to move from one side to the other.
Very little.
Just occasionally somebody smuggled across the border and ended up-- came from Narewka.

We had-- we used to have good contact because those people who worked in the factory where my father was the tool and die maker also came from Narewka, where they had non-Jews who had connections and family in Narewka.
So they worked in Krakow and had family in Narewka.
It-- it was the Krakow Glass Factory.
[SPEAKING POLISH]
Oh, yeah.
Lots, lots of times.
Yeah, I used to hang around his place of work and watch him do his machining.
And so I picked up a little bit of that from just watching him.
Oh yes, uh-huh.
Sure.
I really did.
OK.
